sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 11.43 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.11)

statement.latex: For a finite group$X$, we denote by$k(X)$the number of its conjugacy classes. Is it true that$k(AB) k(A)k(B)$? { L.\,S.\,Kazarin} No, it is not true in general: let$G= a,b a^{30}=b^2=1,\, a^b=a^{-1} D_{60}$be the dihedral group of order$60$, and let$A= a^{10}, ba D_6$and$B= a^6, b D_{10}$. Then$G=AB$, but$G$has$18$conjugacy classes and$A$and$B$only$3$and$4$, respectively. From (P.\,Gallagher, Math...

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory VI_BRIDGING; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
